# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Noise Margin Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A TTL logic family has the following specifications: $V_{OH(min)} = 2.7\,V$, $V_{OL(max)} = 0.4\,V$, $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0\,V$, $V_{IL(max)} = 0.8\,V$. Compute both noise margins and the total static noise immunity.

### Step 1: Identify the given parameters
We are given the four corner voltage parameters of the TTL family.

### Step 2: Apply the HIGH-state noise margin formula
$$
NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}
$$
$$
NM_H = 2.7\,V - 2.0\,V
$$
$$
NM_H = 0.7\,V
$$

### Step 3: Apply the LOW-state noise margin formula
$$
NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}
$$
$$
NM_L = 0.8\,V - 0.4\,V
$$
$$
NM_L = 0.4\,V
$$

### Step 4: Compute the total noise immunity
$$
NM_{total} = NM_H + NM_L
$$
$$
NM_{total} = 0.7\,V + 0.4\,V
$$
$$
NM_{total} = 1.1\,V
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The TTL gate can tolerate up to 0.7 V of positive noise on a HIGH signal and up to 0.4 V of positive noise on a LOW signal before logic corruption occurs. Notice that the noise immunity is **asymmetric** — TTL is more sensitive to noise on the LOW side.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fan-Out Calculation (Two Limiting States)

**Problem Statement:**
For a 74LS00 NAND gate: $I_{OH} = -0.4\,mA$, $I_{OL} = 8\,mA$, $I_{IH} = 20\,\mu A$, $I_{IL} = -0.4\,mA$. Compute the fan-out.

### Step 1: Extract current magnitudes
We use absolute values since the datasheet uses mixed sign conventions.

### Step 2: Compute HIGH-state fan-out
$$
FO_H = \left\lfloor \frac{\vert I_{OH} \vert}{\vert I_{IH} \vert} \right\rfloor
$$
$$
FO_H = \left\lfloor \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}\,A}{20 \times 10^{-6}\,A} \right\rfloor
$$
$$
FO_H = \left\lfloor 20 \right\rfloor = 20
$$

### Step 3: Compute LOW-state fan-out
$$
FO_L = \left\lfloor \frac{\vert I_{OL} \vert}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} \right\rfloor
$$
$$
FO_L = \left\lfloor \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}\,A}{0.4 \times 10^{-3}\,A} \right\rfloor
$$
$$
FO_L = \left\lfloor 20 \right\rfloor = 20
$$

### Step 4: Take the minimum
$$
FO = \min(FO_H, FO_L) = \min(20, 20) = 20
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The 74LS00 output can reliably drive **20 standard 74LS loads** without violating the $V_{OH}$/$V_{OL}$ specifications. In this case, both states give the same answer, but in general they differ and the minimum must be used.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Mixed-Family Fan-Out (Level Translation Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A 74LS00 (TTL) output drives five 74HC00 (CMOS) inputs. The relevant parameters are: TTL: $I_{OL} = 8\,mA$, $I_{OH} = -0.4\,mA$. CMOS (HC) input: $I_{IH} = 1\,\mu A$, $I_{IL} = -1\,\mu A$. Compute the actual fan-out margin.

### Step 1: Compute HIGH-state ratio
$$
FO_H = \frac{\vert I_{OH} \vert}{\vert I_{IH} \vert} = \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 400
$$

### Step 2: Compute LOW-state ratio
$$
FO_L = \frac{\vert I_{OL} \vert}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 8000
$$

### Step 3: Actual fan-out
$$
FO = \min(400, 8000) = 400
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** Driving 5 CMOS loads from a TTL output is **trivially safe** (5 ≪ 400). However, the real bottleneck when mixing TTL↔CMOS at the same $V_{CC}$ is the **voltage-level compatibility**, not the current capability — the TTL $V_{OH(min)} = 2.7\,V$ may fail to reach the CMOS $V_{IH(min)} = 3.5\,V$ for HC-type CMOS at 5 V, requiring a **pull-up resistor** to fix the logic-level translation issue.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Propagation Delay Across a Logic Chain

**Problem Statement:**
A signal passes through three inverters in cascade. Each inverter has $t_{PHL} = 5\,ns$ and $t_{PLH} = 7\,ns$. The interconnecting wires add 1.5 ns of delay per stage. Find the worst-case total propagation delay from input of inverter 1 to output of inverter 3.

### Step 1: Average per-gate delay
$$
t_{pd,gate} = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2} = \frac{5 + 7}{2} = 6\,ns
$$

### Step 2: Total delay through 3 gates
$$
t_{pd,total} = 3 \times t_{pd,gate} = 3 \times 6 = 18\,ns
$$

### Step 3: Add interconnect delays
$$
t_{pd,interconnect} = 3 \times 1.5 = 4.5\,ns
$$

### Step 4: Grand total
$$
t_{pd,grand} = 18 + 4.5 = 22.5\,ns
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** In deep-submicron designs (e.g., 28 nm FPGAs), interconnect delay often **dominates** gate delay. This is why modern place-and-route tools spend more effort minimizing wirelength than minimizing gate count.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Power-Delay Product (Figure-of-Merit)

**Problem Statement:**
A CMOS gate operates at $V_{DD} = 3.3\,V$, has $C_L = 50\,fF$, and $t_{pd} = 100\,ps$. The switching frequency is $f = 100\,MHz$. Compute the dynamic power, energy per transition, and PDP.

### Step 1: Dynamic power
$$
P = C_L V_{DD}^2 f = (50 \times 10^{-15})(3.3)^2(100 \times 10^{6})
$$
$$
P = (50 \times 10^{-15})(10.89)(10^{8})
$$
$$
P = 54.45 \times 10^{-6}\,W = 54.45\,\mu W
$$

### Step 2: Energy per transition
$$
E = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = 0.5 \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \times 10.89
$$
$$
E = 272.25 \times 10^{-15}\,J = 272.25\,fJ
$$

### Step 3: Power-Delay Product
$$
PDP = P \times t_{pd} = (54.45 \times 10^{-6}) \times (100 \times 10^{-12})
$$
$$
PDP = 5.445 \times 10^{-15}\,J = 5.445\,fJ
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** Modern sub-10 nm CMOS designs target PDPs in the **femtojoule range**. Reducing $V_{DD}$ from 3.3 V to 0.8 V (modern logic levels) cuts the energy by a factor of $(3.3/0.8)^2 \approx 17\times$ — illustrating why modern ICs use very low supply voltages.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper Pattern)]**
**Define the terms Fan-In and Fan-Out. Why is excessive fan-out undesirable in a digital circuit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Fan-In (FI):** It is defined as the number of inputs that a single logic gate is designed to accept. (1 mark)
- **Fan-Out (FO):** It is defined as the maximum number of standard loads (similar logic gate inputs) that the output of a logic gate can reliably drive while maintaining the specified $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$ levels. (1 mark)
- **Why excessive fan-out is undesirable:** As fan-out increases, the load on the output driver increases, leading to (a) degradation of the output voltage levels (VOL rises, VOH falls), (b) increased propagation delay due to higher load capacitance, and (c) possible thermal overload of the output transistor. (1 mark)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper Pattern)]**
**Define propagation delay $t_{PLH}$ and $t_{PHL}$ of a logic gate. What is the significance of the Power-Delay Product?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **$t_{PLH}$ (Propagation delay LOW-to-HIGH):** The time delay measured from the 50% point of the input rising transition to the 50% point of the output rising transition. (1 mark)
- **$t_{PHL}$ (Propagation delay HIGH-to-LOW):** The time delay measured from the 50% point of the input falling transition to the 50% point of the output falling transition. (1 mark)
- **Significance of PDP:** The Power-Delay Product ($PDP = P_{avg} \times t_{pd}$) is the figure of merit for a logic family. It quantifies the energy consumed per switching operation. A lower PDP implies a more energy-efficient technology, which is critical for battery-powered and high-density VLSI designs. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Module 4 Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the concept of standard logic levels. Define the four voltage parameters ($V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $V_{OL}$, $V_{OH}$) and derive expressions for the noise margins $NM_H$ and $NM_L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard TTL gate has the following specifications: $V_{OH(min)} = 2.7\,V$, $V_{OL(max)} = 0.4\,V$, $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0\,V$, $V_{IL(max)} = 0.8\,V$. A noise spike of $+0.6\,V$ is superimposed on a LOW output of 0.3 V. Will the next gate interpret it as a valid LOW? Justify with calculations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Define standard logic levels (1 mark)**
Standard logic levels are the guaranteed voltage ranges at the input/output of a digital IC that are unambiguously interpreted as Logic 0 or Logic 1.

**Step 2: Define the four voltage parameters (2 marks)**
- $V_{IL(max)}$ = maximum input voltage guaranteed to be read as Logic 0
- $V_{IH(min)}$ = minimum input voltage guaranteed to be read as Logic 1
- $V_{OL(max)}$ = maximum output voltage when the gate is driving a Logic 0
- $V_{OH(min)}$ = minimum output voltage when the gate is driving a Logic 1

**Step 3: Diagram description (1 mark)**
The diagram should show the input voltage axis divided into three regions: LOW region (0 to $V_{IL(max)}$), forbidden region ($V_{IL(max)}$ to $V_{IH(min)}$), and HIGH region ($V_{IH(min)}$ to $V_{DD}$). The complementary output regions should be marked.

**Step 4: Derivation of noise margins (3 marks)**
$$
NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}
$$
$$
NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}
$$
The HIGH noise margin is the difference between the minimum guaranteed HIGH output and the minimum input required to be recognized as HIGH. The LOW noise margin is the difference between the maximum input still recognized as LOW and the maximum guaranteed LOW output.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Given values (1 mark)**
$V_{OL(actual)} = 0.3\,V$, noise spike = $+0.6\,V$, so the corrupted LOW signal becomes:
$$
V_{LOW,corrupted} = 0.3\,V + 0.6\,V = 0.9\,V
$$

**Step 2: Compare with $V_{IL(max)}$ (2 marks)**
For the signal to be validly interpreted as LOW, we require:
$$
V_{LOW,corrupted} \le V_{IL(max)} = 0.8\,V
$$
But $0.9\,V > 0.8\,V$, so the corrupted signal **falls in the forbidden region**.

**Step 3: Compute the LOW noise margin (2 marks)**
$$
NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)} = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4\,V
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (2 marks)**
Since the noise spike of $+0.6\,V$ exceeds the LOW noise margin of $0.4\,V$, the corrupted LOW signal is **NOT guaranteed** to be interpreted as a valid Logic 0 by the next gate. The next stage may interpret it as a HIGH or an undefined value, causing a **logic fault**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Module 4 Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Define Fan-Out. Derive the expressions for HIGH-state and LOW-state fan-out. Why is the worst-case (minimum) value taken as the final fan-out rating? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The datasheet of a 74LS family gate gives: $I_{OH} = -400\,\mu A$, $I_{OL} = 8\,mA$, $I_{IH} = 20\,\mu A$, $I_{IL} = -0.36\,mA$. Calculate the DC fan-out. The same output is now required to drive 30 identical loads. Will the output voltage levels remain within specification? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Define Fan-Out (1 mark)**
Fan-Out is the maximum number of standard logic-gate inputs that the output of a single logic gate can reliably drive without violating the specified output voltage levels.

**Step 2: Derive HIGH-state fan-out (2 marks)**
The output can source at most $|I_{OH}|$ in the HIGH state. Each load input draws $|I_{IH}|$. Hence:
$$
FO_H = \left\lfloor \frac{\vert I_{OH} \vert}{\vert I_{IH} \vert} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 3: Derive LOW-state fan-out (2 marks)**
The output can sink at most $|I_{OL}|$ in the LOW state. Each load input injects $|I_{IL}|$. Hence:
$$
FO_L = \left\lfloor \frac{\vert I_{OL} \vert}{\vert I_{IL} \vert} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4: Justification of worst-case (2 marks)**
The pull-up and pull-down transistors inside the gate have **different drive strengths** and **different current ratings**. The fan-out must be safe in **both** logic states simultaneously. Whichever state gives the smaller limit will fail first, so we take:
$$
FO = \min(FO_H, FO_L)
$$
Averaging or summing is physically incorrect because the gate must work in both states at all times.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Compute $FO_H$ (2 marks)**
$$
FO_H = \left\lfloor \frac{400 \times 10^{-6}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 20 \rfloor = 20
$$

**Step 2: Compute $FO_L$ (2 marks)**
$$
FO_L = \left\lfloor \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.36 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 22.22 \rfloor = 22
$$

**Step 3: Worst-case fan-out (1 mark)**
$$
FO = \min(20, 22) = 20
$$

**Step 4: Analysis for 30 loads (2 marks)**
The guaranteed fan-out is **20**, but the requirement is **30 loads**, which is **50% higher** than the safe limit. Driving 30 loads will:
- Exceed the HIGH-state current budget → $V_{OH}$ will **drop below** the 2.7 V minimum
- Exceed the LOW-state current budget → $V_{OL}$ will **rise above** the 0.4 V maximum
- Increase propagation delay due to higher effective load capacitance
- Therefore, the output levels will **NOT** remain within TTL specifications. A **buffer** (e.g., 74LS244) must be inserted, or a higher-drive gate (e.g., 74LS244 or 74F00) must be used.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** Treating $I_{OH}$ as positive when it is conventionally negative (current flowing OUT of the output). Always use **absolute values** or be extremely careful with sign.
> 2. **Averaging Fan-Out:** Writing $FO = (FO_H + FO_L)/2$. This is **WRONG**. The correct formula is $FO = \min(FO_H, FO_L)$.
> 3. **Confusing Fan-In with Fan-Out:** Fan-In refers to the number of inputs a gate **has**; Fan-Out refers to the number of inputs a gate **can drive**. Marks are deducted if these are interchanged.
> 4. **Forgetting 50% Measurement Points:** In propagation delay problems, delays are measured between the **50% transition points** of input and output waveforms, not at the start or end of transitions. Drawing waveforms without these points loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing Logic Families Without Checking $V_{OH}$ vs $V_{IH}$:** When TTL drives CMOS at the same $V_{CC}$, current is not the only problem — voltage level compatibility is. A pull-up resistor is often needed. Examiners specifically test this trap.
> 6. **Skipping the Unit Conversion:** Leaving currents in mixed $\mu A$ and $mA$ without converting to a common unit (e.g., amperes) is a frequent mark-loser in numerical problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Standard Logic Levels** are guaranteed voltage ranges ($V_{IL(max)}$, $V_{IH(min)}$, $V_{OL(max)}$, $V_{OH(min)}$) — not exact voltages. The output and input specifications are matched by **overlap** to ensure interoperability.
- **Noise Margins** quantify immunity: $NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}$ and $NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}$. Larger noise margin = more robust system. CMOS generally has better noise margins than TTL.
- **Current parameters** use mixed sign conventions: $I_{OH}$ and $I_{IH}$ are often negative (current flows OUT of the input/output pin for TTL); $I_{OL}$ and $I_{IL}$ are often positive (current flows IN). Always check the datasheet and use absolute values in fan-out formulas.
- **Fan-In** = number of inputs a gate accepts internally (a property of the gate structure, usually 2, 3, 4, or 8). **Fan-Out** = number of standard loads an output can drive (a property of the output driver strength).
- **Fan-Out is the MINIMUM** of $FO_H = |I_{OH}/I_{IH}|$ and $FO_L = |I_{OL}/I_{IL}|$ — never the average or sum.
- **Propagation Delay** has two directional components: $t_{PHL}$ (HIGH-to-LOW) and $t_{PLH}$ (LOW-to-HIGH). Average $t_{pd} = (t_{PHL} + t_{PLH})/2$ is the datasheet rating.
- **50% measurement rule:** Propagation delays are always measured at the 50% transition point of the input and output waveforms, not at 10% or 90% levels.
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** is the figure of merit for a logic family. $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_{pd}$. CMOS PDP scales with $V_{DD}^2$, which is why modern ICs use sub-1 V supplies.
- **Dynamic Power in CMOS:** $P = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. Reducing $V_{DD}$ is the most effective way to cut power dissipation.
- **TTL↔CMOS interface issues:** When driving CMOS from TTL at the same $V_{CC}$, a **pull-up resistor** is often required to bring $V_{OH}$ up to the CMOS $V_{IH(min)}$ threshold.
- **Excessive fan-out** causes: voltage-level degradation, increased delay, overheating, and possible logic malfunction.
- **Real-world speed bottleneck:** In modern nanometer CMOS, **interconnect (wire) delay** dominates **gate (transistor) delay** — this is why physical design (placement & routing) is critical in VLSI.
- **Standard fan-out** for most TTL families is 10; for 74LS it is 20; for 74F and 74AS it can be 20–60; for 74HC CMOS, the DC fan-out is extremely high (hundreds) due to negligible input current, but **AC fan-out** (limited by capacitive loading) is the practical limit.
- **KCL must hold:** Sum of all input currents at fan-out N must not exceed $|I_{OH}|$ (HIGH state) or $|I_{OL}|$ (LOW state), otherwise $V_{OH}$ or $V_{OL}$ will be violated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Consideration. TTL: Basic Working Principle of a TTL NAND Gate - Totem Pole and Open Collector Gate Output Configurations - Tri-State Logic - Characteristics of a TTL NAND Gate. CMOS: Basic Working Principle of a CMOS Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT303 - LOGIC CIRCUITS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Noise Consideration. TTL: Basic Working Principle of a TTL NAND Gate - Totem Pole and Open Collector Gate Output Configurations - Tri-State Logic - Characteristics of a TTL NAND Gate. CMOS: Basic Working Principle of a CMOS Inverter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Noise Consideration in Digital Logic Gates

### Formal Definition
**Noise** is any unwanted voltage fluctuation superimposed on a logic signal that may cause the receiving gate to misinterpret the intended logic level. **Noise Margin (NM)** is the quantitative measure of a logic family's robustness against such disturbances, defined as the difference between the guaranteed output voltage level of a driving gate and the minimum/maximum input voltage level required by the receiving gate for reliable operation.

The two standard DC noise margins are:

- **Noise Margin High (NMH)** = $V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}$
- **Noise Margin Low (NML)** = $V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Noise margin is the most frequently tested topic from this module. Always state both NMH and NML separately and relate them to the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) curve.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a logic gate's input thresholds as the walls of a tunnel, and the output voltage as a train carrying a logic value. **Noise** is like wind pushing the train off the track. The wider the tunnel (i.e., the larger the noise margin), the stronger the wind the train can survive without derailing. A robust logic family (like CMOS) has wide tunnels; a fragile one has narrow tunnels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (TTL 74-series):**
> - $V_{OH(min)} = 2.7\text{ V}$, $V_{OL(max)} = 0.4\text{ V}$
> - $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0\text{ V}$, $V_{IL(max)} = 0.8\text{ V}$
> - Resulting $\text{NMH} = 0.7\text{ V}$, $\text{NML} = 0.4\text{ V}$

---

## 1.2 TTL — Basic Working Principle of a TTL NAND Gate

### Formal Definition
**Transistor-Transistor Logic (TTL)** is a digital logic family built using **bipolar junction transistors (BJTs)** and resistors on a single integrated circuit. A **TTL NAND gate** uses a **multi-emitter input transistor** that replaces the conventional diode AND input structure, providing higher speed and better noise immunity.

The standard TTL NAND has three functional stages:
1. **Input Stage** — multi-emitter NPN transistor performing the AND logic.
2. **Phase-Splitter Stage** — a single transistor that produces two complementary drive signals for the totem-pole output.
3. **Output Stage** — either a **totem-pole** push-pull stage or an **open-collector** stage.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a water-tap (the BJT) controlled by multiple levers (the input emitters). The water flows only when **all** levers are pulled (both inputs HIGH) — this is the AND behavior at the input. A second tap (phase splitter) directs water in opposite directions to two final taps (the output transistors), which push the water up to the tank (HIGH) or drain it to the ground (LOW).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key TTL Input Transistor Function:**
> - When any input is LOW → the multi-emitter transistor is forward-biased → base current is diverted away from Q2 → output is HIGH.
> - When ALL inputs are HIGH → the input transistor operates in reverse-active mode → base current drives Q2 ON → output is LOW.

---

## 1.3 TTL Output Configurations

### (a) Totem-Pole Output
A **totem-pole output** uses two stacked NPN transistors (Q3 and Q4) in a push-pull arrangement, with an active pull-up (Q3) and an active pull-down (Q4). One of them is always ON, providing low output impedance in both logic states.

### (b) Open-Collector Output
An **open-collector output** has only a single pull-down transistor at the output; the collector is left unconnected (open). An **external pull-up resistor** must be connected to $V_{CC}$ for proper operation. This configuration enables **wired-AND** connections of multiple outputs.

### Intuitive Analogy
- **Totem-Pole:** Like a double-acting hydraulic piston — one cylinder pushes up, the other pushes down. Always active, very fast.
- **Open-Collector:** Like a single drain valve — the gate can only pull down, so you must add an external spring (pull-up resistor) to pull it up.

---

## 1.4 Tri-State Logic (Three-State Logic)

### Formal Definition
A **tri-state (3-state) buffer/gate** has three possible output states: **logic HIGH (1)**, **logic LOW (0)**, and a **high-impedance (Hi-Z)** state. In the Hi-Z state, the output is effectively **disconnected** from the circuit, allowing multiple devices to share a common bus without contention.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a tri-state buffer as a **railway switch** controlled by a signal. When the control (Enable) is ACTIVE, the train (data) moves onto the main track. When the control is INACTIVE, the switch disconnects the track completely — the train is "in limbo" (Hi-Z) and cannot collide with trains from other branches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tri-State Truth Table (Active-High Enable):**
> | Enable | Data | Output |
> |:---:|:---:|:---:|
> | 0 | X | Hi-Z |
> | 1 | 0 | 0 |
> | 1 | 1 | 1 |

---

## 1.5 CMOS Inverter — Basic Working Principle

### Formal Definition
**CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)** uses a pair of complementary MOSFETs — a **PMOS pull-up network** and an **NMOS pull-down network** — connected in series between $V_{DD}$ and GND. A **CMOS inverter** is the fundamental CMOS logic gate, in which the PMOS source is tied to $V_{DD}$ and the NMOS source is tied to GND, with their gates tied together as the input and their drains tied together as the output.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of the CMOS inverter as a **double-gate valve system** on a water pipe: one gate opens to the **upstream reservoir** ($V_{DD}$) and the other opens to the **drain** (GND). The gates are mechanically linked so they always work in opposite directions. Pressing the lever (input HIGH) opens the drain (output LOW); releasing it (input LOW) closes the drain and opens the reservoir (output HIGH).

> [!NOTE]
> **CMOS Static Power:** In a steady state, **no current flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND** because either the PMOS or the NMOS is OFF. This gives CMOS its **near-zero static power dissipation**, making it the dominant technology for low-power VLSI.

---

## 1.6 Characteristics of a TTL NAND Gate

The key performance metrics of a TTL NAND gate (standard 74-series) are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{CC}$ | **5.0** | V |
| Power Dissipation (per gate) | $P_D$ | **10** | mW |
| Propagation Delay (avg) | $t_{pd}$ | **10** | ns |
| Fan-Out | $N$ | **10** | — |
| Noise Margin High | $NM_H$ | **0.7** | V |
| Noise Margin Low | $NM_L$ | **0.4** | V |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Speed-Power Product (PDP)** of a gate is defined as:
> $$\text{PDP} = P_D \times t_{pd}$$
> It is measured in **picojoules (pJ)** and is a Figure-of-Merit (FoM) for comparing logic families. A **lower PDP is better**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Noise Margin — The Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)

The VTC of any logic inverter plots $V_{out}$ versus $V_{in}$. Four critical points are defined on this curve:

- $V_{OL(max)}$ — maximum output voltage when output is LOW.
- $V_{IL(max)}$ — maximum input voltage still recognized as logic 0.
- $V_{IH(min)}$ — minimum input voltage still recognized as logic 1.
- $V_{OH(min)}$ — minimum output voltage when output is HIGH.

**Two important regions of the VTC:**
- For $V_{in} < V_{IL}$: $V_{out} = V_{OH}$ (stable HIGH region).
- For $V_{in} > V_{IH}$: $V_{out} = V_{OL}$ (stable LOW region).
- Between $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$: **transition region** (unstable, gain > 1).

### Why Noise Margin Matters
- A **positive noise margin** (NMH > 0 and NML > 0) guarantees that even if noise of magnitude up to the margin is added to the signal, the receiving gate will still interpret the correct logic level.
- If noise exceeds the margin, the input can cross into the transition region, where a small perturbation can flip the output — a **logic error**.

---

## 2.2 TTL NAND Gate — Detailed Operational Stages

### Stage 1: Multi-Emitter Input Transistor (Q1)
- Q1 is an NPN transistor with multiple emitters, each acting as the input terminal.
- If any emitter is LOW, the base-emitter junction is forward-biased, current flows out of the emitter, and Q1 is in **saturation**.
- If all emitters are HIGH, all base-emitter junctions are reverse-biased, current flows from the base into the base-collector junction, and Q1 operates in **reverse-active mode**.

### Stage 2: Phase-Splitter Transistor (Q2)
- Q2's base receives current from Q1's collector.
- When Q2 is ON, it pulls current from Q4's base (turns Q4 ON) AND its collector voltage drops (turns Q3 OFF).
- When Q2 is OFF, the opposite happens.

### Stage 3: Output Stage (Totem-Pole or Open-Collector)
- In **totem-pole**, Q3 (active pull-up) and Q4 (active pull-down) alternate.
- A diode D1 is added to ensure Q3 and Q4 are never ON simultaneously (preventing **shoot-through current**).

---

## 2.3 Totem-Pole vs Open-Collector — Engineering Trade-offs

| Feature | Totem-Pole | Open-Collector |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| External Pull-up Resistor | Not needed | **Required** |
| Output HIGH current source | Active (Q3) | Passive (resistor) |
| Wired-AND Connection | Not possible | **Possible** |
| Switching Speed | **Fast** | Slower (RC time constant) |
| Power Dissipation | Lower (active pull-up) | Higher in HIGH state |
| Bus Driving Capability | Limited (no paralleling) | **Excellent (parallelable)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Explain the difference between totem-pole and open-collector outputs of a TTL gate, with one practical application of each."*

---

## 2.4 Tri-State Logic — Bus Architecture

A tri-state buffer uses a control input (Enable, $\bar{E}$, or $OE$). When **disabled**, both the pull-up and pull-down transistors in the output stage are switched OFF, leaving the output node floating. This allows **multiple tri-state devices to drive a common bus**, but only **one device may be enabled at a time**.

**Applications:**
- Microprocessor data buses.
- Memory and I/O sharing.
- Bidirectional data lines.

---

## 2.5 CMOS Inverter — Detailed Analysis

### Transistor Operation by Region

| Input $V_{in}$ | NMOS State | PMOS State | Output $V_{out}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $V_{in} = 0$ (LOW) | Cutoff | Linear (Triode) | $V_{DD}$ (HIGH) |
| $V_{in} = V_{DD}$ (HIGH) | Linear (Triode) | Cutoff | 0 (LOW) |
| $V_{in} = V_M$ (threshold) | Saturation | Saturation | Switching region |

> [!NOTE]
> **VTC Regions (CMOS Inverter):**
> - **Region A:** PMOS in saturation, NMOS in cutoff → output = $V_{DD}$.
> - **Region B:** PMOS in saturation, NMOS in saturation → high-gain transition.
> - **Region C:** PMOS in cutoff, NMOS in saturation → output = 0.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\text{NMH} = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}$ | Noise margin for logic HIGH | Volts |
| 2 | $\text{NML} = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}$ | Noise margin for logic LOW | Volts |
| 3 | $\text{PDP} = P_D \cdot t_{pd}$ | Speed-Power Product (Figure of Merit) | Joules |
| 4 | $r_{DS(on)} = \dfrac{1}{k_n'(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{T})}$ | MOSFET on-resistance (triode) | Ohms |
| 5 | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$ | CMOS switching threshold | Volts |
| 6 | $I_D = k_n'(W/L)[(V_{GS}-V_T)V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2/2]$ | NMOS triode current | Amps |
| 7 | $I_D = \dfrac{k_n'}{2}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ | NMOS saturation current | Amps |
| 8 | $P_{static} \approx 0$ | CMOS static power (ideal) | Watts |
| 9 | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | CMOS dynamic power | Watts |
| 10 | $t_{pd} = 0.69 R_{on} C_L$ | Propagation delay estimate | Seconds |
| 11 | $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$, $k_p' = \mu_p C_{ox}$ | Process transconductance parameters | A/V² |
| 12 | Fan-out $= \min(I_{OH}/I_{IH},\, I_{OL}/I_{IL})$ | TTL fan-out limit | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value notation** in handwritten or typed math (e.g., $|V_{TP}|$) to avoid confusing it with the table cell separator.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of These Concepts

- **Noise Margin** is the foundation of **signal integrity** in digital system design — critical for high-speed PCB layout, FPGA design, and long-distance serial links.
- **TTL** dominated the 1970s–80s era of digital design; understanding TTL aids in **legacy system maintenance** and as a baseline for teaching bipolar logic.
- **CMOS** is the **backbone of modern VLSI** — every smartphone SoC, microcontroller, and FPGA uses CMOS gates. The zero-static-power principle is why your laptop battery lasts hours, not minutes.
- **Tri-State Logic** is the **enabling technology for buses** — without it, multiple CPU peripherals could not share the same data lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of CMOS Switching Threshold $V_M$

The switching threshold $V_M$ is the point on the VTC where $V_{in} = V_{out}$ and both transistors are in **saturation**. Equating the NMOS and PMOS drain currents at this point:

**Step 1:** Write the saturation current for NMOS:
$$I_{DN,sat} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{TN})^2$$

where $k_n = k_n' \cdot (W/L)_n$ and $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} = V_M$.

**Step 2:** Write the saturation current for PMOS (using $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$):
$$I_{DP,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the KCL condition at the output node (steady state, no output current):
$$I_{DN,sat} = I_{DP,sat}$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions:
$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root of both sides (choosing the positive root):
$$\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{TP}\vert)$$

**Step 6:** Isolate $V_M$ terms on the left and constants on the right:
$$\sqrt{k_n}\,V_M + \sqrt{k_p}\,V_M = \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)$$

Wait — let us carefully rearrange. Move the second term on the right to the left:

$$V_M(\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}) = \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)$$

> **Correction on sign convention:** Standard textbook gives the equation as $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$ — the sign comes from the convention that PMOS threshold is negative ($V_{TP} < 0$), and $|V_{TP}| = -V_{TP}$.

**Step 7:** Factor out $\sqrt{k_n}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical CMOS switching threshold equation**. For a **symmetric inverter** where $k_n = k_p$ and $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$, it simplifies to $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of CMOS Dynamic Power Dissipation

**Step 1:** The load capacitor $C_L$ at the output node must be charged and discharged once per clock cycle.

**Step 2:** Energy drawn from supply during charging (from 0 to $V_{DD}$):
$$E_{charge} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V_{DD}\,i(t)\,dt = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3:** Energy stored in capacitor after charging:
$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4:** Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging:
$$E_{dissipated,charge} = E_{charge} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 5:** During discharge, the energy $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ stored in $C_L$ is dissipated in the NMOS.

**Step 6:** Total energy dissipated per switching cycle:
$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 7:** Multiplying by switching frequency $f$ and activity factor $\alpha$:
$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$$

This is the famous **$CV^2f$ power** equation, the dominant power component in modern CMOS.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TTL NAND Gate State Analysis

Let A, B be the inputs and Y be the output of a 2-input TTL NAND gate. Assume standard 7400 series TTL levels.

| Case | A | B | Q1 Mode | Q2 Mode | Q3 | Q4 | Output Y |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | L | L | Saturation (FB) | OFF | ON | OFF | **H (≈3.6 V)** |
| 2 | L | H | Saturation (FB) | OFF | ON | OFF | **H** |
| 3 | H | L | Saturation (FB) | OFF | ON | OFF | **H** |
| 4 | H | H | Reverse-Active | ON (sat) | OFF | ON | **L (≈0.2 V)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Output is HIGH if **any** input is LOW, and LOW only if **all** inputs are HIGH — this is the **NAND** truth table.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CMOS Inverter VTC Region Analysis

Using $V_{TN} = 0.7\text{ V}$, $|V_{TP}| = 0.7\text{ V}$, $V_{DD} = 5\text{ V}$:

| Region | $V_{in}$ Range | NMOS Mode | PMOS Mode | $V_{out}$ | Slope (Gain) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 0 to 0.7 V | Cutoff | Triode | 5.0 V (HIGH) | 0 |
| B | 0.7 to 1.4 V | Saturation | Saturation | Transition | Steep (high gain) |
| C | 1.4 to 2.1 V | Saturation | Triode | Transition | Steep |
| D | 2.1 to 5.0 V | Triode | Cutoff | 0 V (LOW) | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact transition points depend on the **W/L ratios** of the transistors. A symmetric design gives a sharp, symmetric VTC centered at $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.

---

## 3.5 Noise Margin Calculation — Worked Example

**Problem:** A TTL gate has $V_{OH} = 2.7$ V, $V_{OL} = 0.4$ V, $V_{IH} = 2.0$ V, $V_{IL} = 0.8$ V. Calculate NMH and NML.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the NMH formula:
$$\text{NMH} = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)} = 2.7\text{ V} - 2.0\text{ V} = 0.7\text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the NML formula:
$$\text{NML} = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)} = 0.8\text{ V} - 0.4\text{ V} = 0.4\text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation:
- The HIGH signal can tolerate **up to 0.7 V** of positive-going noise before being misinterpreted.
- The LOW signal can tolerate **up to 0.4 V** of negative-going noise before being misinterpreted.
- The asymmetry is a well-known **weakness of TTL**; CMOS is symmetric (NMH ≈ NML ≈ $V_{DD}/4$).

---

## 3.6 Fan-Out Calculation — Worked Example

**Problem:** A TTL driver has $I_{OH} = 400\,\mu\text{A}$, $I_{OL} = 16\text{ mA}$, $I_{IH} = 40\,\mu\text{A}$, $I_{IL} = 1.6\text{ mA}$. Calculate the fan-out.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Fan-out for HIGH state (current sourcing):
$$\text{Fan-out}_H = \frac{I_{OH}}{I_{IH}} = \frac{400\,\mu\text{A}}{40\,\mu\text{A}} = 10$$

**Step 2:** Fan-out for LOW state (current sinking):
$$\text{Fan-out}_L = \frac{I_{OL}}{I_{IL}} = \frac{16\text{ mA}}{1.6\text{ mA}} = 10$$

**Step 3:** Overall fan-out is the minimum:
$$\text{Fan-out} = \min(10,\, 10) = 10$$

**Step 4:** Result: This TTL gate can reliably drive **10** similar gates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TTL NAND Gate — Block Functional Architecture

> **Note on Diagrams:** Since Mermaid cannot render the precise multi-emitter BJT structure with bias networks, the diagrams below present a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the signal path and logic transformation at each stage.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input A] --> N1[Multi-Emitter Transistor Q1]
    B[Input B] --> N1
    N1 --> N2[Phase Splitter Q2 Base]
    N2 --> N3[Phase Splitter Q2 Collector]
    N2 --> N4[Phase Splitter Q2 Emitter]
    N3 --> N5[Upper Output Transistor Q3]
    N4 --> N6[Lower Output Transistor Q4]
    N5 --> Y[Output Y]
    N6 --> Y
    N7[Diode D1] --- N5
    N7 --- N6
    VCC[VCC = 5V] --> N8[Pull-up Resistor R1]
    N8 --> N2
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**
- A and B enter the **multi-emitter transistor (Q1)** — the input AND function.
- Q1's collector current feeds the **phase splitter (Q2)** base.
- Q2 splits the signal into two **complementary** paths (collector and emitter).
- Q3 (upper, active pull-up) and Q4 (lower, active pull-down) form the **totem-pole**.
- **Diode D1** ensures Q3 and Q4 never turn ON simultaneously.

---

## 4.2 CMOS Inverter — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    VIN[Input Voltage VIN] --> G1[Common Gate Node]
    G1 --> P1[PMOS Transistor]
    G1 --> N1[NMOS Transistor]
    P1 --> OUT[Output Voltage VOUT]
    N1 --> OUT
    VDD[VDD Supply] --> P1
    GND[Ground GND] --> N1
    CL[Load Capacitor CL] --- OUT
    CL --- GND
```

**Interpretation:**
- The single input $V_{in}$ drives both gates simultaneously.
- When $V_{in}$ is LOW → PMOS conducts (channel forms), NMOS OFF → $V_{out}$ pulled to $V_{DD}$.
- When $V_{in}$ is HIGH → NMOS conducts, PMOS OFF → $V_{out}$ pulled to 0.
- The **load capacitor** $C_L$ represents the parasitic + load capacitance that must be charged/discharged.

---

## 4.3 Tri-State Buffer — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    D[Data Input D] --> BF[Tri-State Buffer Core]
    E[Enable Input E] --> BF
    BF --> O[Output Y]
    S1[Logic HIGH Path] --- BF
    S2[Logic LOW Path] --- BF
    S3[High Impedance Path] --- BF
```

**Three operational states:**
1. **HIGH state:** E = 1, D = 1 → output actively driven HIGH by pull-up transistor.
2. **LOW state:** E = 1, D = 0 → output actively driven LOW by pull-down transistor.
3. **Hi-Z state:** E = 0 → both transistors OFF, output floating.

---

## 4.4 Open-Collector Output — Wired-AND Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    G1[Gate 1 Open Collector] --> BUS[Common Bus Line]
    G2[Gate 2 Open Collector] --> BUS
    G3[Gate 3 Open Collector] --> BUS
    RU[External Pull-up Resistor RU] --> BUS
    RU --> VCC[VCC = 5V]
    BUS --> Y[Combined Output Y]
```

**Working Principle:**
- Each gate has only a pull-down transistor at its output.
- The pull-up resistor is **shared** by all gates on the bus.
- Output Y is HIGH only if **all** gate outputs are HIGH (i.e., all pull-down transistors OFF).
- Output Y is LOW if **any** gate output is LOW.
- This is the **Wired-AND** (or more accurately, **Wired-AND-Logic**) function: $Y = \overline{A \cdot B \cdot C \cdot \ldots}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wired-AND with open-collector outputs** is a classic KTU question worth 5–7 marks. The key insight is that the AND logic is achieved **physically through the wired connection**, not through a separate AND gate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the terms Noise Margin High (NMH) and Noise Margin Low (NML) for a digital logic gate. Why is it important to have positive noise margins in a logic family?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **NMH** is defined as the difference between the minimum guaranteed HIGH output voltage of a driving gate and the minimum input voltage that the receiving gate will still recognize as a valid HIGH.
  $$\text{NMH} = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}$$

- **NML** is defined as the difference between the maximum input voltage that the receiving gate will still recognize as a valid LOW and the maximum guaranteed LOW output voltage of the driving gate.
  $$\text{NML} = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}$$

- **Importance:** Positive noise margins guarantee that any noise voltage of magnitude up to the margin can be tolerated without causing a logic error. If either margin were zero or negative, the gate would be extremely susceptible to noise-induced false switching, making reliable digital communication impossible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the order of subtraction. **NMH = $V_{OH} - V_{IH}$** (output minus input threshold), and **NML = $V_{IL} - V_{OL}$** (input threshold minus output). Reversing the sign is a very common error worth **−1 mark**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a tri-state logic gate? Mention any two applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **tri-state (3-state) logic gate** is a special digital circuit that has three possible output states:
1. **Logic HIGH (1)** — actively driven high.
2. **Logic LOW (0)** — actively driven low.
3. **High-impedance (Hi-Z)** — output is effectively disconnected (floating).

The transition to Hi-Z is controlled by an additional **Enable (EN)** input. When the enable signal is inactive, both internal pull-up and pull-down transistors are switched OFF, isolating the output.

**Two Applications:**
- **Bus multiplexing:** Multiple devices (memory, I/O, CPU) can share a common data bus, with only one device enabled at a time to avoid bus contention.
- **Bidirectional I/O pins:** A single pin can be used for both input and output, common in microcontrollers and FPGAs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **2-input TTL NAND gate** with totem-pole output. Discuss the role of the multi-emitter input transistor and the phase-splitter stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the totem-pole and open-collector output configurations of a TTL gate. Explain how open-collector outputs are used to implement a **wired-AND** connection. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The 2-input TTL NAND gate with totem-pole output consists of:
- **Q1:** Multi-emitter NPN input transistor (2 emitters for 2 inputs).
- **Q2:** NPN phase-splitter transistor.
- **Q3 and Q4:** NPN transistors in totem-pole configuration at the output.
- **D1:** Level-shift diode between Q3 base and Q4 collector.
- **R1, R2, R3, R4:** Biasing and load resistors.
- $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$ supply.

**Operation Analysis (5 marks):**

**Case 1: Both inputs A and B are HIGH (≈ 5 V).**
- All base-emitter junctions of Q1 are reverse-biased.
- $V_{CC}$ supplies current through R1 to Q1's base, which flows into Q1's collector and then into Q2's base.
- Q2 turns **ON** (saturates).
- Q2's collector voltage drops to a low value, so Q3 (whose base is connected to Q2's collector through D1) turns **OFF**.
- Q2's emitter voltage rises, driving Q4's base high, turning Q4 **ON** (saturates).
- Q4 sinks the output current to ground.
- **Output Y = LOW (≈ 0.2 V).**

**Case 2: Either input A or B (or both) is LOW (≈ 0.2 V).**
- The corresponding base-emitter junction of Q1 becomes forward-biased.
- Current from $V_{CC}$ through R1 flows out through the LOW input emitter to ground.
- Q1's collector current is essentially zero, so Q2 has no base drive and turns **OFF**.
- Q2's collector voltage rises toward $V_{CC}$, so Q3 (with D1) turns **ON** (active pull-up).
- Q2's emitter is at 0 V, so Q4 has no base drive and remains **OFF**.
- **Output Y = HIGH (≈ 3.6 V).**

**Truth Table Realization:** $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ — this is the **NAND** function.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** A common mistake is to claim that the multi-emitter transistor performs the AND function by itself. **In reality**, Q1 only routes the base current; the actual logic is realized by the **entire network's switching behavior**. Avoid writing vague statements like "Q1 does AND"; instead, explain the current paths in detail.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Totem-Pole Output | Open-Collector Output |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Output Transistors | Q3 (active pull-up) + Q4 (active pull-down) | Only Q4 (pull-down) |
| External Components | None | Pull-up resistor required |
| Output HIGH Source | Active (Q3) | Passive (resistor) |
| Switching Speed | Fast | Slower (RC delay) |
| Wired-AND | Not possible | Possible |
| Power in HIGH state | Lower | Higher ($I$ through $R_L$) |
| Bus Driving | Cannot parallel outputs | Can parallel outputs |

**Wired-AND Connection (4 marks):**
- In open-collector configuration, the collector of Q4 is left unconnected internally and brought out as the output pin.
- An **external pull-up resistor** $R_L$ is connected between the output and $V_{CC}$.
- When multiple open-collector outputs are tied to a common line, they all share the same pull-up resistor.
- The output node Y is HIGH only if **all** open-collector transistors are OFF (i.e., all gates are outputting HIGH internally).
- If **any** gate outputs LOW, its transistor turns ON, sinking current through $R_L$ and pulling Y to ground.
- The resulting function is: $Y = \overline{A \cdot B \cdot C \cdot \ldots}$ (the AND of all gate outputs, then inverted).
- This is called **Wired-AND logic** because the AND function is achieved through the physical wiring, not through an explicit AND gate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Some students incorrectly call the wired-AND function "Wired-OR." It is **Wired-AND** (with an implicit inversion at the output node due to the active LOW nature of open-collector sinking). Do not confuse the two.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **basic working principle of a CMOS inverter** with a neat circuit diagram. Discuss its voltage transfer characteristic (VTC) in detail, identifying the five regions of operation and the switching threshold $V_M$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With relevant equations, derive the expression for the **switching threshold $V_M$** of a CMOS inverter. Calculate $V_M$ for $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{TN} = 0.5$ V, $|V_{TP}| = 0.6$ V, and $(W/L)_n = 2$, $(W/L)_p = 4$, with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$ and $\mu_p C_{ox} = 25\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A CMOS inverter consists of:
- A **PMOS transistor** (pull-up) with source connected to $V_{DD}$, drain to output, gate to input.
- An **NMOS transistor** (pull-down) with source connected to GND, drain to output, gate to input.
- Both gates tied together as the input node; both drains tied together as the output node.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- **When $V_{in} = 0$ (LOW):** $V_{GS,p} = -V_{DD} < V_{TP}$, so PMOS is ON; $V_{GS,n} = 0 < V_{TN}$, so NMOS is OFF. The output is connected to $V_{DD}$ through the PMOS → $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ (HIGH).
- **When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$ (HIGH):** $V_{GS,p} = 0$, PMOS is OFF; $V_{GS,n} = V_{DD} > V_{TN}$, NMOS is ON. The output is connected to GND through the NMOS → $V_{out} = 0$ (LOW).
- **In steady state**, one transistor is always OFF, so **no static current flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND**.

**VTC Five Regions (2 marks):**
1. **Region I (0 ≤ $V_{in}$ < $V_{TN}$):** NMOS cutoff, PMOS triode. $V_{out} = V_{DD}$.
2. **Region II ($V_{TN}$ ≤ $V_{in}$ < $V_{M}$):** NMOS saturation, PMOS saturation. High-gain transition.
3. **Region III ($V_M$):** Both in saturation. $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$. Switching threshold.
4. **Region IV ($V_M$ < $V_{in}$ ≤ $V_{DD} - |V_{TP}|$):** NMOS triode, PMOS saturation. Continued transition.
5. **Region V ($V_{DD} - |V_{TP}|$ < $V_{in}$ ≤ $V_{DD}$):** NMOS triode, PMOS cutoff. $V_{out} = 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** A common mistake is to claim the output swings "from 0 to 5 V" without specifying the supply voltage. Always **explicitly state $V_{DD}$** and identify all **five regions** of the VTC for full credit. Skipping Region II or IV costs **−1 mark each**.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (4 marks):** [As derived in Section 3.1]

$$V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the transconductance parameters:
$$k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \cdot (W/L)_n = 50 \times 2 = 100\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$$
$$k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} \cdot (W/L)_p = 25 \times 4 = 100\,\mu\text{A/V}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio:
$$\sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{100}} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the $V_M$ equation:
$$V_M = \frac{0.5 + 1 \cdot (3.3 + 0.6)}{1 + 1} = \frac{0.5 + 3.9}{2} = \frac{4.4}{2} = 2.2\text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: The switching threshold is at 2.2 V, which is exactly $V_{DD}/2 = 1.65$ V... 

> **Correction:** With the given parameters, $V_M = 2.2$ V, which is **above** $V_{DD}/2$. This indicates a **slightly PMOS-stronger-than-NMOS characteristic** in terms of the threshold placement, but since $k_p/k_n = 1$ exactly, the difference arises only from the asymmetric threshold voltages $V_{TN} \neq |V_{TP}|$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** A frequent error is to incorrectly compute the $k_p/k_n$ ratio. Students often forget to **multiply the process parameter by the W/L ratio**. The process parameter $\mu C_{ox}$ alone is not $k$; you must compute $k = \mu C_{ox} \cdot (W/L)$. Losing this step costs **−1 to −2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Noise Margin:** $\text{NMH} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$; $\text{NML} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$. CMOS has symmetric (≈ $V_{DD}/4$ each); TTL is asymmetric (NMH = 0.7 V, NML = 0.4 V).
- **TTL NAND — Input stage:** Multi-emitter BJT Q1. If any input LOW → Q1 saturated → Q2 OFF → output HIGH. If all inputs HIGH → Q1 reverse-active → Q2 ON → output LOW.
- **TTL NAND — Phase splitter (Q2):** Splits the signal into two complementary paths — one drives the upper totem-pole transistor (Q3) and the other drives the lower one (Q4).
- **TTL NAND — Totem-pole output:** Q3 (active pull-up) and Q4 (active pull-down) never ON simultaneously (Diode D1 ensures this). Provides low output impedance and high speed.
- **TTL NAND — Open-collector output:** Only Q4 present; **external pull-up resistor required** to $V_{CC}$. Enables **Wired-AND** logic: $Y = \overline{A \cdot B \cdot C \cdot \ldots}$
- **Tri-state logic:** Three output states — HIGH, LOW, **Hi-Z**. Used in **bus multiplexing**. Only one device enabled at a time.
- **CMOS inverter:** PMOS (pull-up) + NMOS (pull-down), complementary operation. **Static power ≈ 0**. Dynamic power $= \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ (the famous $CV^2f$ law).
- **Switching threshold $V_M$:** Equals $V_{DD}/2$ for symmetric CMOS ($k_n = k_p$ and $V_{TN} = |V_{TP}|$); shifts asymmetrically otherwise.
- **Fan-out of TTL:** $\min(I_{OH}/I_{IH},\, I_{OL}/I_{IL})$ — typically 10 for 74-series.
- **Speed-Power Product (PDP):** $P_D \times t_{pd}$, in **picojoules** — lower is better.
- **Diode D1 in TTL:** Ensures Q3 and Q4 never both conduct, preventing **shoot-through current** that would cause a massive current spike from $V_{CC}$ to GND.
- **Wired-AND with open-collector:** Logic is in the **wiring**, not in any active gate; reduces component count and is useful for **interrupt lines** and **bus signaling**.
- **CMOS power hierarchy:** $P_{static} \approx 0 < P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f < P_{short-circuit}$ (during brief switching transients).
- **Tri-state enable polarity:** Active-HIGH enable ($EN = 1$ → buffer active) is the most common, but **active-LOW enable** ($\overline{OE} = 0$ → output enabled) is also widely used in real ICs (e.g., 74LS125).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of TTL & CMOS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT303 - LOGIC CIRCUITS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Comparison of TTL & CMOS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In digital electronics, two dominant logic families have shaped the design of combinational and sequential circuits: **TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic)** and **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Logic)**. Their comparison is a mandatory topic in KTU's *PCEBT303 – Logic Circuits and Design* syllabus (Module 4: Logic Families and Interfacing) because engineers must select the appropriate family for a given application based on speed, power, noise immunity, fan-out, and integration density. > [!NOTE] > **Definition – TTL:** A bipolar junction transistor (BJT)-based logic family in which the basic switching element is a multi-emitter transistor at the input stage, followed by transistor amplification stages. It operates on a single **+5 V** supply. > > **Definition – CMOS:** A logic family built from complementary pairs of **n-channel** and **p-channel MOSFETs**, where a logic '0' turns on the NMOS (pull-down) and turns off the PMOS, while a logic '1' does the opposite. It is the backbone of virtually all modern VLSI/ULSI chips. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two ways to control a water tap: - **TTL is like a manual hand-pump tap**: the water (current) is always being pushed through the pipes even when the tap is "off" (a small but constant current leaks). It is fast to respond (water gushes out the moment you turn it), but it wastes energy continuously. - **CMOS is like a sensor-activated electric valve**: it consumes almost no power when idle (closed circuit) because the complementary MOSFETs ensure one is always OFF. However, every time the valve opens or closes (switching), it briefly uses energy — like pouring a small bucket of water per transition. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway:** TTL wastes power *constantly* (static dissipation). CMOS wastes power *only during switching* (dynamic dissipation), making it the universal choice for battery-powered and high-density VLSI circuits. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Bold - **Power Dissipation (P_D):** TTL ≈ **10 mW/gate**, CMOS ≈ **10 nW/gate (static)** - **Propagation Delay (t_pd):** TTL ≈ **1–10 ns**, CMOS ≈ **1–50 ns** (modern sub-ns) - **Fan-out:** TTL ≈ **10**, CMOS ≈ **>50** - **Noise Margin:** TTL ≈ **0.4 V**, CMOS ≈ **1.5 V (at 5 V V_DD)** - **Supply Voltage:** TTL = **5 V ± 0.25 V**, CMOS = **3 V to 15 V (4000 series)** - **Operating Temperature:** TTL = **0 °C to 70 °C**, CMOS = **−55 °C to 125 °C** - **Speed–Power Product:** TTL ≈ **100 pJ**, CMOS ≈ **1 pJ** (lower is better) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step **TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic)** 1. The input stage uses a **multi-emitter NPN transistor**. When any input is LOW, the corresponding emitter-base junction is forward-biased, pulling current from the base of the next transistor — output goes HIGH. 2. When *all* inputs are HIGH, the multi-emitter transistor enters reverse-active mode, current flows into the base of the phase-splitter transistor — output goes LOW. 3. A **totem-pole output stage** (two stacked NPNs) provides active pull-up and active pull-down, giving TTL its characteristic low output impedance and high switching speed. 4. Because the output transistors always conduct some current (a defined path from V_CC to ground exists in one of the states), TTL has high *static* power consumption. **CMOS (Complementary MOS)** 1. Each CMOS gate consists of a **pull-up network (PMOS)** and a **pull-down network (NMOS)** that are logical complements of each other. 2. In steady state (input HIGH or LOW), *exactly one* of the networks is OFF. Therefore, the direct path from V_DD to ground is **broken** → static current is essentially zero (only leakage, in the **nA** range). 3. Power is consumed only when both transistors are momentarily in saturation during the input transition — this is the **dynamic power**. 4. The output capacitance (C_L) must charge/discharge through the ON MOSFET, giving CMOS its speed-power trade-off. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---|---| | **Static Power Dissipation (CMOS)** | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Dominated by sub-threshold & junction leakage (W) | | **Dynamic Power Dissipation (CMOS)** | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C_L$ = load capacitance, $f$ = clock frequency (W) | | **Short-Circuit Power (CMOS)** | $P_{SC} = I_{SC} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Power dissipated during input rise/fall transitions (W) | | **Total CMOS Power** | $P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic} + P_{SC}$ | Sum of all dissipation components (W) | | **TTL Power Dissipation** | $P_{TTL} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC}$ | Essentially constant regardless of frequency (W) | | **Propagation Delay** | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$ | Average of high-to-low and low-to-high delays (s) | | **Speed–Power Product (Figure of Merit)** | $SPP = t_{pd} \cdot P_D$ | Lower value = better logic family (Joules) | | **Noise Margin HIGH** | $NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}$ | Logic 1 noise tolerance (V) | | **Noise Margin LOW** | $NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}$ | Logic 0 noise tolerance (V) | | **Fan-out (CMOS)** | $FO = \dfrac{I_{OH}}{I_{IH}}$ | Limited by drive current vs. input leakage (unitless) | | **Fan-out (TTL)** | $FO = \dfrac{I_{OL}}{I_{IL}} \approx 10$ | Empirically limited to ~10 for standard TTL | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** You will almost always be asked to *calculate dynamic power* for a CMOS gate operating at a given frequency and load capacitance. Memorize $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **TTL** dominated the 1970s–80s era and is still found in legacy industrial control systems, hobbyist kits (e.g., 7400-series), and some high-speed glue logic. - **CMOS** is the foundation of all modern microprocessors (Intel, AMD, ARM), FPGAs, ASICs, SRAM, and almost every IoT sensor. Its low power enables billions of transistors on a single die. - **Mixed-signal designs** still require CMOS-to-TTL or TTL-to-CMOS interfacing (level shifting) — a common KTU exam question. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Dynamic Power Dissipation of a CMOS Inverter The energy drawn from the supply $V_{DD}$ to charge the load capacitance $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$ is: $$E = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dV = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2}$$ **Logical interpretation:** The supply delivers a charge $Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$ at voltage $V_{DD}$, hence the energy supplied is $V_{DD} \cdot Q$. However, only **half** of this energy is stored on $C_L$ (the useful part); the other half is dissipated as heat in the **PMOS pull-up transistor** during charging. This is a unique CMOS characteristic. Now, if the gate switches at a frequency $f$ with switching activity factor $\alpha$ (probability of a 0→1 transition per clock cycle), the average dynamic power is: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{dynamic} &= \alpha \cdot f \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step transition logic:** - $f$ represents the number of full charge–discharge cycles per second. - $\alpha$ accounts for the fact that not every clock cycle causes a transition. - $C_L$ represents the total output capacitance (intrinsic + wiring + load). - $V_{DD}^{2}$ shows the quadratic dependence on supply voltage — the single most important lever for low-power CMOS design (e.g., ARM cores use $V_{DD} \approx 0.7$ V). ### 3.2 Comparison Matrix (KTU Board-Ready Table) | Parameter | **TTL (Standard 74xx Series)** | **CMOS (Standard 74HC / CD4000)** | |---|---|---| | **Active Device** | Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) | nMOS + pMOS (complementary pair) | | **Supply Voltage (V_CC / V_DD)** | **+5 V ± 0.25 V** (fixed) | **3 V to 15 V** (wide range) | | **Logic Levels (V_OH / V_OL)** | 2.7 V / 0.4 V | ≈ V_DD / ≈ 0 V | | **Static Power Dissipation** | **~10 mW/gate** (always ON) | **~10 nW/gate** (essentially zero) | | **Dynamic Power** | Negligible change with $f$ | $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$ (dominant) | | **Propagation Delay (t_pd)** | **1 – 10 ns** (faster) | **1 – 50 ns** (HC-series comparable) | | **Fan-out** | **~10** (input current load) | **>50** (input is capacitive) | | **Noise Margin** | ~0.4 V | ~1.5 V (at V_DD = 5 V) | | **Input Impedance** | Low (~4 kΩ) | Very high (>10¹² Ω) | | **Output Drive Current** | High (~16 mA sink/source) | Moderate (HC: ±4 mA, AHC: ±8 mA) | | **Speed–Power Product** | ~100 pJ | ~1 pJ (10–100× better) | | **Integration Density** | Low (transistors are bulky) | Very high (micron/nano scale) | | **Susceptibility to ESD** | Low | **High** (gate oxide damage) | | **Latch-up** | Not applicable | Possible (parasitic SCR) | | **Operating Temperature** | 0 °C to 70 °C | −55 °C to 125 °C (wider) | | **Cost per Gate (2024)** | Higher | Very low (dominant in industry) | | **Typical Use Today** | Legacy glue logic, hobby projects | All microprocessors, FPGAs, ASICs | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A CMOS inverter is clocked at $f = 100$ MHz with a load capacitance $C_L = 10$ pF and supply $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V. Switching activity $\alpha = 0.25$. Calculate dynamic power. $$ \begin{aligned} P_{dynamic} &= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f \\ &= 0.25 \times (10 \times 10^{-12}) \times (3.3)^{2} \times (100 \times 10^{6}) \\ &= 0.25 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 10.89 \times 10^{8} \\ &= 0.25 \times 10.89 \times 10 \times 10^{-4} \\ &= 2.7225 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \\ &\approx \mathbf{2.72 \text{ mW}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Insight:** Notice how halving $V_{DD}$ (from 3.3 V to 1.65 V) would reduce power to **¼** of the original — this is the dominant reason modern CPUs aggressively lower their supply voltage. ### 3.4 TTL-to-CMOS Interfacing — Voltage Level Translation A common KTU question: *A 5 V TTL output must drive a 3.3 V CMOS input. What is the problem?* - TTL $V_{OH(min)} = 2.7$ V is **less than** CMOS $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0$ V → works at 5 V, but margin is poor. - More critically, if TTL is at 5 V and CMOS runs at 3.3 V, TTL HIGH (5 V) **overstresses** the CMOS gate oxide. A level-shifter or a pull-up resistor to 3.3 V is required. A simple solution uses a **74HCT-series** (High-speed CMOS, TTL-compatible inputs) which accepts TTL logic levels directly. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TTL NAND Gate (74LS00) ```mermaid graph LR inputA[A Input] --> multiEmitter[Multi-Emitter BJT] inputB[B Input] --> multiEmitter multiEmitter --> phaseSplit[Phase-Splitter Transistor] phaseSplit --> driverLow[Lower Output NPN Q3] phaseSplit --> driverHigh[Upper Output NPN Q4] driverHigh --> outputNode[Output Y] driverLow --> outputNode outputNode --> load[External Load] vccNode[Vcc = 5V] --> driverHigh gndNode[GND] --> driverLow style multiEmitter fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600 style phaseSplit fill:#b4d8ff,stroke:#003366 style outputNode fill:#c8f7c5,stroke:#1f7a1f ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CMOS Inverter ```mermaid graph LR vin[Input Vin] --> pmosGate[PMOS Gate] vin --> nmosGate[NMOS Gate] pmosGate --> pmosDrain[PMOS Drain] nmosGate --> nmosDrain[NMOS Drain] pmosDrain --> outputNode[Output Vout] nmosDrain --> outputNode vddNode[Vdd Supply] --> pmosSource[PMOS Source] gndNode[GND] --> nmosSource[NMOS Source] loadCap[Load Capacitance CL] --- outputNode style pmosGate fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style nmosGate fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099 style outputNode fill:#c8f7c5,stroke:#1f7a1f style loadCap fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#806600 ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Trade-off Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD start[Choose Logic Family] --> q1{Is power
critical?} q1 -- Yes --> cmos[Use CMOS] q1 -- No --> q2{Need ultra-high
speed?} q2 -- Yes --> ttl[Use TTL LS or
ECL for >GHz] q2 -- No --> q3{Integration
density needed?} q3 -- Yes --> cmos q3 -- No --> ttl cmos --> note1[Note: 74HCT bridges
TTL and CMOS] ttl --> note1 style start fill:#e0e0e0 style cmos fill:#c8f7c5 style ttl fill:#ffd6d6 style note1 fill:#fff2a8 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** TTL and CMOS use fundamentally different physical structures (BJT vs. MOSFET), so the diagrams above are **functional block representations** of the current/voltage paths rather than physical layout cross-sections. The key visual takeaway is that TTL has a *continuous current path* through its output stage, whereas CMOS has a *broken* path in steady state. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Why does CMOS logic consume negligible static power compared to TTL?* **Model Answer:** In steady state, the CMOS gate's input is either HIGH or LOW, which keeps either the PMOS pull-up network or the NMOS pull-down network completely OFF. Hence, the direct path from V_DD to ground is **broken**, and only tiny leakage currents (in nA) flow — giving static power ≈ 10 nW/gate. In contrast, TTL's totem-pole output stage always has a conducting path, dissipating ~10 mW/gate continuously. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Define the term "Speed-Power Product" and state its typical value for CMOS and TTL families.* **Model Answer:** Speed-Power Product (SPP) is the figure of merit of a logic family, defined as the product of propagation delay (t_pd) and power dissipation (P_D). It is measured in **picojoules (pJ)**. Typical values are: $$SPP_{TTL} \approx 100 \text{ pJ}, \quad SPP_{CMOS} \approx 1 \text{ pJ}$$ A lower SPP indicates a more efficient logic family. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Compare the TTL and CMOS logic families in terms of (i) basic building block, (ii) supply voltage, (iii) power dissipation, (iv) fan-out, (v) noise margin, and (vi) propagation delay. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 50$ pF at a clock frequency of $f = 25$ MHz. The supply is $V_{DD} = 5$ V, and the switching activity factor is $\alpha = 0.2$. Calculate the dynamic power dissipation. If the supply is reduced to 3.3 V, by what factor does the power change? *(7 marks)* **Model Solution to (a):** | # | Parameter | TTL (74LS) | CMOS (74HC) | |---|---|---|---| | i | Basic Building Block | BJT (multi-emitter input) | nMOS + pMOS pair | | ii | Supply Voltage | **5 V ± 0.25 V** | **2 V – 6 V (HC)**, up to 15 V (4000) | | iii | Power Dissipation | ~2 mW/gate (static, freq-independent) | ~10 nW static, dynamic dominates | | iv | Fan-out | ~20 (LS) | >50 (capacitive loading only) | | v | Noise Margin | ~0.4 V | ~1.5 V (at 5 V V_DD) | | vi | Propagation Delay | ~9.5 ns (LS) | ~8 ns (HC) | **Model Solution to (b):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{dynamic\,1} &= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD1}^{\,2} \cdot f \\ &= 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 5^2 \times 25 \times 10^{6} \\ &= 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 \times 25 \times 10^{6} \\ &= 0.2 \times 31250 \times 10^{-6} \\ &= 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \\ &= \mathbf{6.25 \text{ mW}} \end{aligned} $$ For $V_{DD2} = 3.3$ V: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{dynamic\,2} &= 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.3^2 \times 25 \times 10^{6} \\ &= 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \times 10.89 \times 25 \times 10^{6} \\ &= 2.7225 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \\ &\approx \mathbf{2.72 \text{ mW}} \end{aligned} $$ **Ratio of reduction:** $$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{V_{DD1}^2}{V_{DD2}^2} = \frac{25}{10.89} \approx 2.296$$ Hence, reducing the supply from 5 V to 3.3 V cuts the dynamic power by a factor of **~2.3**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the working of a **CMOS NAND gate** with a neat circuit diagram. Show that its static power dissipation is essentially zero. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A TTL gate drives 10 similar TTL gates. The input low current is $I_{IL} = 0.4$ mA and the input high current is $I_{IH} = 20$ μA. The output low current is $I_{OL} = 8$ mA and the output high current is $I_{OH} = 0.4$ mA. Determine the fan-out for both logic levels. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution to (a):** A 2-input CMOS NAND gate consists of **two PMOS transistors in parallel** (pull-up network) and **two NMOS transistors in series** (pull-down network). - **Case 1: A = 0, B = 0 (any input LOW):** At least one PMOS is ON, both NMOS are OFF → output = V_DD (HIGH). No DC path exists. - **Case 2: A = 1, B = 0:** Upper PMOS (controlled by A) OFF, lower PMOS (controlled by B) ON; upper NMOS ON, lower NMOS OFF → output pulled HIGH. No DC path. - **Case 3: A = 1, B = 1 (both HIGH):** Both PMOS OFF, both NMOS ON → output pulled to GND. Again, no DC path. - **Case 4: Switching transition:** Brief moment when both pull-up and pull-down conduct → causes a small short-circuit current pulse, contributing to **dynamic** power. In **all steady states**, the V_DD-to-GND path is broken. Therefore, **static power ≈ 0** (limited only by leakage, in nA). **Model Solution to (b):** Fan-out in the LOW state: $$FO_L = \frac{I_{OL(max)}}{I_{IL(max)}} = \frac{8 \text{ mA}}{0.4 \text{ mA}} = 20$$ Fan-out in the HIGH state: $$FO_H = \frac{I_{OH(max)}}{I_{IH(max)}} = \frac{0.4 \text{ mA}}{0.02 \text{ mA}} = 20$$ The actual fan-out is the **worst-case (minimum)** of the two: $$FO = \min(20, 20) = \mathbf{20}$$ Since the gate must drive only 10 other gates, the design is **safe with margin**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the activity factor $\alpha$:** Many students write $P = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ without $\alpha$. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this. > 2. **Mixing up $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$:** Always write the standard TTL values as 2.7 V / 0.4 V, not "5 V / 0 V". > 3. **Confusing fan-out limits:** Fan-out is the *minimum* of HIGH-state and LOW-state ratios — many students take the maximum by mistake. > 4. **Static vs. dynamic power:** In CMOS, static is **negligible**; in TTL, dynamic is **negligible**. Reversing this loses marks. > 5. **Forgetting units in dynamic power calculations:** Always end with **W or mW**, not just a number. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **TTL** uses **BJTs**, **CMOS** uses **complementary MOSFETs**. - ✅ **Static Power:** TTL ≈ 10 mW; CMOS ≈ 10 nW (almost zero in steady state). - ✅ **Dynamic Power** of CMOS: $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^{\,2} \, f$ — **quadratic in V_DD**. - ✅ **Speed–Power Product:** CMOS (≈1 pJ) ≪ TTL (≈100 pJ) → CMOS is 10–100× more efficient. - ✅ **Fan-out:** CMOS > 50; TTL ≈ 10–20. - ✅ **Noise Margin:** CMOS (≈1.5 V) is **twice** that of TTL (≈0.4 V). - ✅ **Supply:** TTL fixed at 5 V; CMOS flexible (3 V – 15 V). - ✅ **Modern trend:** CMOS dominates — all microprocessors, FPGAs, and ASICs use CMOS. - ✅ **TTL still alive** in 74LS, 74AS, 74F series; **HCT series** bridges TTL-CMOS voltage levels. - ✅ **ESD sensitivity:** CMOS gates are vulnerable — handle with anti-static precautions. - ✅ **Latch-up** is a CMOS-specific failure caused by parasitic thyristor (SCR) action. - ✅ **For exam:** Always quote the **dynamic power formula**, the **fan-out calculation steps**, and the **noise margin definitions** — these are the three most frequently tested points on this topic.

topics.title: Interfacing TTL & CMOS ICs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT303 - LOGIC CIRCUITS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interfacing TTL & CMOS ICs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interfacing TTL and CMOS ICs** is the design technique used to reliably connect (cascade) digital integrated circuits from two different logic families — **Bipolar Transistor-Transistor Logic (TTL)** and **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (CMOS)** — within the same digital system, ensuring that the logic-level voltages, noise margins, and current drive capabilities of the *driving* output are correctly matched to the *receiving* input.

In a mixed-logic printed circuit board, it is extremely common to find a TTL gate controlling a CMOS counter, or a CMOS microcontroller driving a TTL buffer. Because these two families were manufactured with fundamentally different internal transistor physics, their **input switching thresholds**, **output voltage rails**, and **source/sink current capabilities** are not inherently identical. The interfacing discipline guarantees signal integrity across the boundary.

### 1.2 The Four Critical Logic Parameters (Common to Every Family)

To understand interfacing, you must master four input and four output boundary parameters. Every logic IC datasheet specifies these:

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Input LOW voltage max** | $V_{IL(\max)}$ | Highest voltage that is guaranteed to be read as logic **0** |
| **Input HIGH voltage min** | $V_{IH(\min)}$ | Lowest voltage that is guaranteed to be read as logic **1** |
| **Output LOW voltage max** | $V_{OL(\max)}$ | Highest voltage the output sits at when driving **0** |
| **Output HIGH voltage min** | $V_{OH(\min)}$ | Lowest voltage the output sits at when driving **1** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Voltage Translator" Story)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — Two people speaking different languages:**
> Imagine a German engineer (TTL) handing a blueprint to a Japanese architect (CMOS). The German's notebook says "ON = 2.4 V" but the Japanese translator only counts a note as "ON" if it shows at least "3.5 V". The Japanese will *mishear* the German. The **interface circuit** is simply a **translator/interpreter** that bumps the German's 2.4 V up to 5 V so the Japanese understands correctly. The **pull-up resistor** is the interpreter. The **level shifter IC** is the professional human translator.

The central engineering goal is therefore:

$$
V_{OH(\text{DRIVER})} \geq V_{IH(\text{RECEIVER})} \quad \text{AND} \quad V_{OL(\text{DRIVER})} \leq V_{IL(\text{RECEIVER})}
$$

If both conditions hold across every transition, the families are **electrically compatible**. If not, an interface network must be inserted.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Standard Parameter Values (Memorize These)

These are the values you **must memorize** for the 74LS (TTL) and 74HC (CMOS) families operating at **$V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$**.

| Parameter | **74LS TTL** | **74HC CMOS** | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $V_{OH(\min)}$ | **2.7** | **4.9** | V |
| $V_{OL(\max)}$ | **0.5** | **0.1** | V |
| $V_{IH(\min)}$ | **2.0** | **3.5** | V |
| $V_{IL(\max)}$ | **0.8** | **1.5** | V |
| $I_{OH(\max)}$ | **−0.4** | **−4** | mA |
| $I_{OL(\max)}$ | **8** | **4** | mA |
| $I_{IH(\max)}$ | **20** | **1** | µA |
| $I_{IL(\max)}$ | **−0.4** | **1** | µA |
| Noise Margin HIGH ($V_{NH}$) | $2.7 - 2.0 = \mathbf{0.7}$ | $4.9 - 3.5 = \mathbf{1.4}$ | V |
| Noise Margin LOW ($V_{NL}$) | $0.8 - 0.5 = \mathbf{0.3}$ | $1.5 - 0.1 = \mathbf{1.4}$ | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Single Most Critical Number for Interfacing:** Look at $V_{OH(\min)}$ of TTL = **2.7 V** versus $V_{IH(\min)}$ of CMOS = **3.5 V**. The TTL output is *guaranteed* to swing only to 2.7 V, but the CMOS input demands at least 3.5 V. Therefore, **a bare TTL-to-CMOS connection is INCOMPATIBLE in the HIGH state** at 5 V. This is the cornerstone question KTU examiners love.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Interfacing is Needed — The Three Mismatch Categories

When two logic families meet, mismatches can occur in three independent dimensions. The designer must check all three before declaring the system "compatible".

### Category 1 — Voltage-Level Mismatch (Logic Threshold Mismatch)
This is the most fundamental issue. Occurs when the driver's guaranteed HIGH/LOW output voltages do not fall inside the receiver's recognized input HIGH/LOW windows.

$$
\text{Compatible HIGH} \iff V_{OH(\min)}^{\text{DRIVER}} \geq V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{RECEIVER}}
$$

$$
\text{Compatible LOW} \iff V_{OL(\max)}^{\text{DRIVER}} \leq V_{IL(\max)}^{\text{RECEIVER}}
$$

### Category 2 — Current-Drive Mismatch (Fan-Out / Fan-In Mismatch)
Even if voltage levels are valid, the source must be able to **source** enough current in HIGH state and **sink** enough current in LOW state to feed the inputs connected to it. The **DC Fan-Out** formula is:

$$
\text{DC Fan-Out} = \min \left( \frac{I_{OH(\max)}^{\text{DRIVER}}}{I_{IH(\max)}^{\text{RECEIVER}}}, \ \frac{I_{OL(\max)}^{\text{DRIVER}}}{I_{IL(\max)}^{\text{RECEIVER}}} \right)
$$

### Category 3 — Power Supply / Speed Mismatch
CMOS HC devices run from 2 V to 6 V, while TTL strictly requires 5 V ± 0.5 V. A 3.3 V CMOS device cannot reliably drive a 5 V TTL input HIGH without a level shifter, because its $V_{OH(\max)} = 3.3\text{ V} < V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{TTL}} = 2.0\text{ V}$ is *technically* OK, but its noise margin is destroyed.

---

## 2.2 Case Analysis — The Four Canonical Interfacing Scenarios

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests these four cases. Walk through them in order.

### Case A — TTL (5 V) Driving CMOS (5 V)

- **LOW state check:** $V_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} = 0.5\text{ V} \le V_{IL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}} = 1.5\text{ V}$ ✔
- **HIGH state check:** $V_{OH(\min)}^{\text{TTL}} = 2.7\text{ V} \not\ge V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{CMOS}} = 3.5\text{ V}$ ✘
- **Verdict:** **INCOMPATIBLE** in the HIGH state.
- **Solution:** Add an external **pull-up resistor** $R_P$ from the TTL output to $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$. When the TTL output transistor (the upper totem-pole) goes OFF, the resistor pulls the line all the way to 5 V, satisfying CMOS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard value of pull-up resistor:** $R_P = \mathbf{2.2\ k\Omega}$ to $\mathbf{4.7\ k\Omega}$. Lower values waste current in the LOW state (TTL's lower transistor must sink through both $R_P$ and itself to ground); higher values slow the rising edge. KTU expects you to know the *typical* range.

### Case B — CMOS (5 V) Driving TTL (5 V)

- **LOW state check:** $V_{OL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}} = 0.1\text{ V} \le V_{IL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} = 0.8\text{ V}$ ✔
