- **HIGH state check:** $V_{OH(\min)}^{\text{CMOS}} = 4.9\text{ V} \ge V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{TTL}} = 2.0\text{ V}$ ✔
- **Current check:** CMOS can sink $4\text{ mA}$ (HIGH state) — enough for one or two 74LS loads. Fan-out is generally adequate.
- **Verdict:** **DIRECTLY COMPATIBLE** in most cases. No interface components needed.

### Case C — 3.3 V CMOS Driving 5 V TTL

- **HIGH state check:** $V_{OH(\max)}^{\text{3.3V CMOS}} = 3.3\text{ V} \ge V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{TTL}} = 2.0\text{ V}$ ✔ (marginal)
- **Verdict:** Sometimes works in practice, but a **level shifter / voltage translator** (e.g., 74LVTH125, TXS0108E) is recommended for production designs.

### Case D — 5 V TTL Driving 3.3 V CMOS

- **HIGH state check:** $V_{OH(\min)}^{\text{TTL}} = 2.7\text{ V}$. If the 3.3 V CMOS is "5 V tolerant" on its inputs, it accepts up to 5 V, and 2.7 V easily clears its $V_{IH(\min)} \approx 0.7 \times 3.3 = 2.31\text{ V}$ ✔
- **LOW state check:** $0.5\text{ V} \le 0.99\text{ V}$ ✔
- **Verdict:** Compatible **IF** the CMOS inputs are 5 V tolerant. Otherwise, use a series resistor or a level shifter IC.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Purpose | Units |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_{NH} = V_{OH(\min)} - V_{IH(\min)}$ | HIGH-state Noise Margin | V |
| 2 | $V_{NL} = V_{IL(\max)} - V_{OL(\max)}$ | LOW-state Noise Margin | V |
| 3 | $\text{Fan-Out} = \min\!\left( \dfrac{I_{OH(\max)}}{I_{IH(\max)}}, \dfrac{I_{OL(\max)}}{I_{IL(\max)}} \right)$ | Max loads a driver can feed | unitless |
| 4 | $R_{P(\min)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}}}{I_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} + n \cdot I_{IL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}}}$ | Min pull-up allowed | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $R_{P(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{CMOS}}}{I_{IH(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}} + n \cdot I_{OH(\max)}^{\text{TTL}}}$ | Max pull-up (speed limit) | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $t_{r} \approx 0.69 \, R_P \, C_{\text{load}}$ | Rise time at TTL→CMOS node | s |
| 7 | $V_{IH} \approx 0.7 \, V_{DD}$ | CMOS switching threshold (rule of thumb) | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The same interfacing principles govern **microcontroller-to-sensor**, **FPGA-to-DDR memory**, and **5 V industrial PLCs talking to 3.3 V Raspberry Pi GPIO** lines. The exact same four-parameter framework decides whether a translator chip is needed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing the Pull-Up Resistor Range for TTL → CMOS

This is the **most important numerical problem** KTU 2024 can ask on this topic. We must find the allowable range of $R_P$.

### Step 0 — Given / Stated Information

- Supply: $V_{CC} = 5.0\text{ V}$
- TTL driver (74LS04): $V_{OL(\max)} = 0.5\text{ V}$, $I_{OL(\max)} = 8\text{ mA}$, $I_{OH(\max)} = -0.4\text{ mA}$
- CMOS load (74HC04): $V_{IH(\min)} = 3.5\text{ V}$, $I_{IH(\max)} = 1\ \mu\text{A}$, $I_{IL(\max)} = 1\ \mu\text{A}$
- Number of CMOS inputs being driven: $n = 1$

### Step 1 — Establish the LOW-State Current Constraint

When the TTL output is LOW, both the pull-up resistor $R_P$ and any connected CMOS input leak currents flow **into** the TTL output transistor, which must sink the sum.

By Kirchhoff's Current Law at the TTL output node:

$$
I_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} \geq I_{R_P}^{\text{LOW}} + n \cdot I_{IL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}}
$$

Solve for $I_{R_P}^{\text{LOW}}$:

$$
I_{R_P}^{\text{LOW}} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}}}{R_P} \leq I_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} - n \cdot I_{IL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}}
$$

Rearrange to find the **minimum allowable** $R_P$:

$$
R_{P(\min)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}}}{I_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} - n \cdot I_{IL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numbers

$$
R_{P(\min)} = \frac{5.0\text{ V} - 0.5\text{ V}}{8\text{ mA} - 1 \times 1\ \mu\text{A}}
$$

The CMOS input leakage is negligible compared to 8 mA:

$$
R_{P(\min)} \approx \frac{4.5\text{ V}}{8\text{ mA}} = 562.5\ \Omega
$$

### Step 3 — Establish the HIGH-State Voltage Constraint

When the TTL output is HIGH (its upper totem-pole transistor is OFF), the pull-up resistor $R_P$ must deliver enough current to charge any load capacitance AND supply the tiny CMOS input bias currents, while keeping the node voltage $\geq V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{CMOS}}$.

$$
V_{\text{node}} = V_{CC} - R_P \cdot I_{\text{HIGH}} \geq V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{CMOS}}
$$

Solving for the **maximum allowable** $R_P$:

$$
R_{P(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{CMOS}}}{I_{IH(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}} + n \cdot \vert I_{OH(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} \vert}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Numbers

$$
R_{P(\max)} = \frac{5.0\text{ V} - 3.5\text{ V}}{1\ \mu\text{A} + 1 \times 0.4\text{ mA}}
$$

The CMOS input current is negligible:

$$
R_{P(\max)} \approx \frac{1.5\text{ V}}{0.4\text{ mA}} = 3750\ \Omega = 3.75\text{ k}\Omega
$$

### Step 5 — State the Final Answer with Tolerance

The design must satisfy both:

$$
562.5\ \Omega \leq R_P \leq 3750\ \Omega
$$

A safe, standard, **KTU-expected answer** is:

$$
\boxed{R_P = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega \text{ (or } 3.3\text{ k}\Omega \text{), placed between TTL output and } V_{CC = 5\text{ V}}}
$$

This completes the numerical derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: DC Fan-Out of 74HC CMOS Driving 74LS TTL

### Given
- CMOS output: $I_{OH(\max)} = 4\text{ mA}$, $I_{OL(\max)} = 4\text{ mA}$
- TTL input: $I_{IH(\max)} = 20\ \mu\text{A}$, $I_{IL(\max)} = 0.4\text{ mA}$

### Step 1 — HIGH-state limit

$$
N_H = \frac{I_{OH(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}}}{I_{IH(\max)}^{\text{TTL}}} = \frac{4\text{ mA}}{20\ \mu\text{A}} = 200
$$

### Step 2 — LOW-state limit

$$
N_L = \frac{I_{OL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}}}{I_{IL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}}} = \frac{4\text{ mA}}{0.4\text{ mA}} = 10
$$

### Step 3 — DC Fan-Out is the *limiting* (minimum) value

$$
\text{Fan-Out} = \min(200,\ 10) = \boxed{10}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The fan-out is always limited by the *worst-case* state. A common student mistake is to compute only the HIGH-state value and forget that TTL inputs demand **20× more current in the LOW state** than in the HIGH state (because the TTL input is essentially an emitter that *sources* current out of the pin when LOW).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — TTL Driving CMOS via Pull-Up Resistor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["TTL DRIVER 74LS04 OUTPUT"] -->|Vout 0 to 2.7V| nodeB["INTERCONNECTION NODE"]
    nodeR["RP 2.2K PULLUP RESISTOR"] -.->|connected to| nodeB
    nodeV["VCC 5.0V"] --> nodeR
    nodeB -->|Vin 0 to 5.0V| nodeC["CMOS LOAD 74HC04 INPUT"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["GROUND 0V"]
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — CMOS Driving TTL (Direct)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["CMOS DRIVER 74HC04 VCC 5V"] -->|Vout 0.1V to 4.9V| nodeB["DIRECT CONNECTION NODE"]
    nodeB -->|Vin| nodeC["TTL LOAD 74LS04 INPUT"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["GROUND 0V"]
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
```

flowchart LR

    nodeC -->|5V signal back| nodeB

flowchart TB

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**State any two reasons why TTL and CMOS ICs cannot always be directly connected to each other without an interface circuit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Voltage-level mismatch:** The guaranteed HIGH output voltage of standard TTL is $V_{OH(\min)} = 2.7\text{ V}$, while the CMOS input requires $V_{IH(\min)} = 3.5\text{ V}$ at 5 V supply. Thus TTL cannot drive CMOS HIGH without assistance.
2. **Current-drive / fan-out mismatch:** TTL inputs draw $I_{IL} = 0.4\text{ mA}$ in the LOW state, which may exceed the $I_{OL(\max)}$ of certain CMOS families, especially when one CMOS output must drive many TTL inputs.
3. *(any other valid point: power supply difference, switching threshold difference)*

*(1.5 marks per correct reason — 3 marks total)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is the role of a pull-up resistor when interfacing standard TTL to CMOS at $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$? State its typical value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The TTL totem-pole output's HIGH level saturates internally to about 2.7 V because of the voltage drop across the upper output transistor. The CMOS receiver needs at least 3.5 V to register a logic HIGH. The pull-up resistor connects the TTL output node to $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$ so that when TTL's upper transistor turns OFF, the resistor pulls the line all the way up to 5 V — comfortably above the CMOS $V_{IH(\min)}$ of 3.5 V.

Typical value: **$R_P = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$ to $4.7\text{ k}\Omega$**, with $3.3\text{ k}\Omega$ being a very common design choice.

*(1.5 marks role + 1.5 marks typical value)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students frequently quote the *typical* (e.g., 3.4 V) instead of the *guaranteed worst-case* (2.7 V) $V_{OH}$ of TTL. Always use the **datasheet minimum** when checking compatibility — otherwise the design fails in production.
> 2. In the pull-up resistor numerical, do **not** forget to subtract the leakage current of the CMOS input from the TTL's sink capability in the LOW state. Marks are explicitly awarded for the full KCL equation.
> 3. CMOS inputs have a static $I_{IH}$ in micro-amps — neglecting this when computing $R_{P(\max)}$ will lead to an over-optimistic (too high) resistor value and a slow rising edge.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — TTL → CMOS with Pull-Up Design

**(a)** Compare the input and output voltage parameters of standard 74LS TTL and 74HC CMOS families at 5 V. Identify the specific incompatibility between them and explain the role of a pull-up resistor in resolving it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a TTL gate (74LS) driving a single 74HC CMOS gate, derive the allowable range of the pull-up resistor $R_P$ connected between the TTL output and $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$. Use the following data:
TTL: $V_{OL(\max)} = 0.5\text{ V}$, $I_{OL(\max)} = 8\text{ mA}$, $I_{OH(\max)} = 0.4\text{ mA}$.
CMOS: $V_{IH(\min)} = 3.5\text{ V}$, $I_{IH(\max)} = 1\ \mu\text{A}$, $I_{IL(\max)} = 1\ \mu\text{A}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Parameter Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | 74LS TTL | 74HC CMOS | Compatible? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $V_{OH(\min)}$ | 2.7 V | 4.9 V | — |
| $V_{OL(\max)}$ | 0.5 V | 0.1 V | — |
| $V_{IH(\min)}$ | 2.0 V | 3.5 V | — |
| $V_{IL(\max)}$ | 0.8 V | 1.5 V | — |
| $V_{NH}$ | 0.7 V | 1.4 V | — |

**Identification of Incompatibility (2 marks):**
- LOW state: $V_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} = 0.5\text{ V} \le V_{IL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}} = 1.5\text{ V}$ ✔ — **Compatible.**
- HIGH state: $V_{OH(\min)}^{\text{TTL}} = 2.7\text{ V} < V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{CMOS}} = 3.5\text{ V}$ ✘ — **Incompatible.** The TTL HIGH output sits 0.8 V *below* the CMOS HIGH threshold, so the CMOS would interpret a logic 1 as undefined.

**Role of Pull-Up Resistor (1 mark):**
A resistor $R_P$ (≈ 2.2 kΩ) connected from the TTL output to $V_{CC}$ pulls the line up to 5 V when TTL's upper output transistor is OFF, guaranteeing $V_{OH} \approx 5\text{ V} \ge 3.5\text{ V}$ at the CMOS input.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — $R_{P(\min)}$ from LOW-state current limit (3 marks):**

$$
R_{P(\min)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}}}{I_{OL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} - n \cdot I_{IL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}}} = \frac{5.0 - 0.5}{8\text{ mA} - 1\ \mu\text{A}} = \frac{4.5}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 562.5\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — $R_{P(\max)}$ from HIGH-state voltage limit (3 marks):**

$$
R_{P(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{CMOS}}}{I_{IH(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}} + n \cdot \vert I_{OH(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} \vert} = \frac{5.0 - 3.5}{1\ \mu\text{A} + 0.4\text{ mA}} = \frac{1.5}{0.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 3750\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{562.5\ \Omega \le R_P \le 3.75\text{ k}\Omega \implies \text{Choose standard } R_P = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — CMOS → TTL Direct Interfacing & Fan-Out

**(a)** With reference to the 74HC CMOS and 74LS TTL families operating at 5 V, explain why a CMOS output can directly drive a TTL input without any interface components. Verify your answer using the four voltage parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 74HC00 CMOS NAND gate output is used to drive multiple 74LS00 TTL NAND-gate inputs. Compute the maximum DC fan-out of the CMOS gate. Given: CMOS $I_{OH(\max)} = 4\text{ mA}$, $I_{OL(\max)} = 4\text{ mA}$; TTL $I_{IH(\max)} = 20\ \mu\text{A}$, $I_{IL(\max)} = 0.4\text{ mA}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Voltage compatibility check (5 marks):**

- **LOW state:** $V_{OL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}} = 0.1\text{ V} \le V_{IL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}} = 0.8\text{ V}$ → **0.7 V of LOW noise margin**, comfortably satisfied.
- **HIGH state:** $V_{OH(\min)}^{\text{CMOS}} = 4.9\text{ V} \ge V_{IH(\min)}^{\text{TTL}} = 2.0\text{ V}$ → **2.9 V of HIGH noise margin**, vastly over-driven.

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
Because the CMOS output swings almost the entire rail-to-rail (0.1 V to 4.9 V) and the TTL input is happy to accept anything above 2.0 V as HIGH and below 0.8 V as LOW, the two families are **electrically compatible in voltage** when both run from the same 5 V supply. The only remaining question is the current-drive / fan-out, which is addressed in part (b). Hence, **no interface components are required** for CMOS→TTL at 5 V.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — HIGH-state fan-out (3 marks):**

$$
N_H = \frac{I_{OH(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}}}{I_{IH(\max)}^{\text{TTL}}} = \frac{4\text{ mA}}{20\ \mu\text{A}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 200
$$

**Step 2 — LOW-state fan-out (3 marks):**

$$
N_L = \frac{I_{OL(\max)}^{\text{CMOS}}}{I_{IL(\max)}^{\text{TTL}}} = \frac{4\text{ mA}}{0.4\text{ mA}} = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case fan-out (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\text{DC Fan-Out} = \min(200,\ 10) = 10 \text{ TTL loads}}
$$

**Interpretation (bonus 1 mark):** The CMOS output can drive up to **10 standard 74LS TTL inputs**. If more loads are needed, use a CMOS buffer (e.g., 74HC244) to boost the drive.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Standard 74LS TTL parameters at 5 V:** $V_{OH(\min)}=2.7\text{ V}$, $V_{OL(\max)}=0.5\text{ V}$, $V_{IH(\min)}=2.0\text{ V}$, $V_{IL(\max)}=0.8\text{ V}$.
- ✅ **Standard 74HC CMOS parameters at 5 V:** $V_{OH(\min)}=4.9\text{ V}$, $V_{OL(\max)}=0.1\text{ V}$, $V_{IH(\min)}=3.5\text{ V}$, $V_{IL(\max)}=1.5\text{ V}$.
- ✅ **TTL → CMOS at 5 V is INCOMPATIBLE in HIGH state** because $2.7\text{ V} < 3.5\text{ V}$. **Fix:** Add a pull-up resistor of $2.2\text{ k}\Omega$ to $4.7\text{ k}\Omega$ between the TTL output and $V_{CC}$.
- ✅ **CMOS → TTL at 5 V is DIRECTLY COMPATIBLE** because CMOS swings rail-to-rail and easily exceeds the TTL thresholds.
- ✅ **Fan-out formula:** $\min\!\left( \dfrac{I_{OH(\max)}^{\text{DRIVER}}}{I_{IH(\max)}^{\text{LOAD}}}, \ \dfrac{I_{OL(\max)}^{\text{DRIVER}}}{I_{IL(\max)}^{\text{LOAD}}} \right)$ — always take the **worst-case minimum**.
- ✅ **Pull-up resistor range formula:** $R_{P(\min)}$ from LOW-state current sink; $R_{P(\max)}$ from HIGH-state voltage drop.
- ✅ **3.3 V ↔ 5 V** crossing almost always needs a **dedicated level-shifter IC** (e.g., TXS0108E, 74LVTH125).
- ✅ **Always use datasheet *guaranteed worst-case* values** ($V_{OH(\min)}$, $V_{OL(\max)}$, $I_{OL(\max)}$, etc.) — typical values are marketing numbers, design numbers are worst-case.
- ✅ **Noise margins** for HC CMOS (1.4 V) are double those of LS TTL (0.7 V / 0.3 V) — CMOS is inherently more noise-immune.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favourite 14-mark sub-question:** "Derive the range of pull-up resistor for TTL→CMOS" — memorise the two KCL/KVL equations.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favourite 3-mark question:** "Why is a pull-up resistor needed?" — answer in one sentence: *to lift the TTL HIGH output above the CMOS $V_{IH(\min)}$ threshold.*
